## SECTION 1 — Global Pulse (Geopolitics & World Economy)

In the past 24-48 hours, the Middle East has experienced a severe escalation of hostilities following US-Israel military strikes on Iran and subsequent Iranian retaliatory attacks. On February 28, the United States and Israel initiated strikes on Iran, targeting leadership, nuclear and ballistic missile programs, and armed forces. Iran responded on March 1 by launching missiles and drones at Israel, US military bases, and various locations in the UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, Jordan, and Saudi Arabia. This conflict has been described as a "war" that is rapidly spreading through the Middle East, leading to significant disruption in global transportation and logistics, including the suspension of air and ocean freight services in the region. The European Union and Switzerland signed a broad package of agreements on March 2, aiming to deepen their relationship and expand frictionless access to markets, covering sectors from trade and transport to health and energy.

## SECTION 2 — Global News (Complete Continental Coverage)

### Americas
The US-Israel military strikes against Iran and Iran's subsequent retaliations have elevated geopolitical tensions. The US Department of State issued an emergency security update on March 2, urging all Americans in 15 Middle Eastern nations, including Iran, Israel, and the UAE, to depart immediately via commercial means due to serious safety risks. President Trump indicated the conflict could last "four to five weeks".

### Europe
Europe is increasingly drawn into the Middle East conflict, leading to defensive actions to protect military bases and efforts to evacuate citizens. On March 2, European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and Swiss Confederation President Guy Parmelin signed new agreements to deepen bilateral ties, covering food safety, electricity markets, and health initiatives. France's President Macron stated on March 2 that France would increase its number of nuclear warheads, emphasizing the need to strengthen its deterrent in response to new global challenges. A British base in Cyprus was reportedly hit by a drone strike on March 2, prompting Greece to deploy F-16s to bolster air defense.

### Asia-Pacific
Japan's RIKEN Center for Computational Science and Singapore's National Quantum Computing Hub formalized a three-year collaboration on March 2 to develop hybrid quantum-classical computing platforms, aiming to integrate high-performance computing with quantum technologies. A total lunar eclipse, visible across parts of Asia-Pacific, is anticipated in the early hours of March 3.

### Africa
On March 1, the 2026 Southern African Development Community (SADC) Sustainable Energy Week concluded in Victoria Falls, Zimbabwe, with Member States committing to accelerating clean energy deployment and strengthening regional integration. The African Union's 11th Africa Day of School Feeding, held on March 1, highlighted that school feeding programs now reach over 86 million children across Africa. The UN Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (MONUSCO) mandate will be reviewed by the Security Council in March, addressing efforts to implement a ceasefire between the DRC government and the M23 rebel group.

### Oceania
The Oceania Cycling Confederation announced on November 13, 2025, that Australia will host seven Oceania Championships in 2026, including BMX Racing, Mountain Bike, and Road Championships in Brisbane, Queensland, and other locations. The 13th Oceania Academy biennial conference on Cerebral Palsy and other Childhood-onset Disabilities is scheduled to be held in Hobart, Tasmania, Australia from March 4-7, 2026. Cook Islanders in Dubai described the environment as "scary" following explosions in Gulf states over the weekend due to the Middle East conflict.

## SECTION 3 — Gulf & MENA Region

The Gulf and MENA region is experiencing widespread instability due to military strikes. On February 28, the US and Israel launched attacks on Iran, killing several Iranian leaders, including Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. In retaliation, Iran fired missiles and drones at Israel, the UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, Jordan, and Saudi Arabia on March 1. This has led to the closure of airspace in Israel, Jordan, and Iraq, and a "Depart Now" alert from the US Department of State for Americans in 15 nations, including the UAE, where US government personnel are sheltering in place. Humanitarian organizations, including the Iranian Red Crescent Society and Magen David Adom in Israel, are on high alert and responding to urgent needs.

## SECTION 4 — Technology and the Future

Quantum Computing Inc. (QUBT) announced on March 3 that it has completed the acquisition of Luminar Semiconductor and opened a new quantum chip foundry. The company also reported its first commercial sales of quantum cybersecurity solutions, including a contract with a major US bank, and is collaborating with NASA. Researchers at UC Santa Barbara received a National Science Foundation grant on March 2 to study theoretical aspects of quantum cryptography, aiming to understand how quantum computers can protect privacy and security. Florida International University (FIU) researchers also developed a quantum-safe encryption system, announced on March 2, designed to protect digital content from future cyberattacks, performing 10-15% better than comparable advanced encryption techniques in tests.

## SECTION 9 — Energy, Climate & Environment Watch

Global oil prices surged dramatically due to the escalating conflict in the Middle East and concerns over supply disruptions, particularly through the Strait of Hormuz, which accounts for approximately one-fifth of global oil shipments. Brent crude oil rose to $79.65 USD/Bbl on March 3, up 2.46% from the previous day, while WTI crude oil reached $72.41 USD/Bbl, up 1.65%. OPEC+ agreed on Sunday to increase output by 206,000 barrels per day in April, which was less than anticipated, contributing to market volatility.

The state also plans to strengthen education infrastructure with new engineering colleges and residential schools.

## SECTION 18 — Deep Analysis of Most Important Breaking News

### US-Israel Strikes on Iran and Regional Escalation

**What Happened:** On February 28, 2026, the United States and Israel launched coordinated military strikes on Iran, targeting its leadership, nuclear program, ballistic missile facilities, and armed forces. This operation resulted in casualties, including the death of Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, confirmed by Iranian state media on March 1. In retaliation, Iran launched multiple missile and drone attacks on March 1 against Israel, US military bases in the region, and targets in the UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, Jordan, and Saudi Arabia.

**Root Causes:** The strikes by the US and Israel were reportedly aimed at inducing regime change in Iran and addressing concerns regarding its nuclear and ballistic missile programs. US President Trump stated that Iran posed a threat to "core national security interests of the US" and that diplomatic agreements to curtail Iran's nuclear program had failed. The Iranian regime was also facing extensive internal protests earlier in 2026 due to a weakened economy and struggling infrastructure.

**Historical Background:** Tensions between the US, Israel, and Iran have been a long-standing feature of Middle Eastern geopolitics, frequently revolving around Iran's nuclear ambitions, its regional influence, and support for various proxy groups. Previous US administrations have engaged in sanctions and military posturing, while Israel has consistently viewed Iran as an existential threat. The current conflict follows a period of heightened internal unrest in Iran and a weakening of some of its regional allies.

**Key Actors:** The primary actors are the United States, Israel, and Iran. Other involved parties include various Gulf states (UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia) and Jordan, which have been targeted by Iranian retaliation. Hezbollah in Lebanon has also engaged in attacks on Israel. European nations are involved through their military bases in the region, facing threats and working on citizen evacuations.

**Strategic Importance:** This escalation carries immense strategic importance, risking a broader regional conflagration. It has led to widespread disruption of global transportation and logistics, including the effective stalling of shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for global oil supplies. The conflict has caused crude oil prices to skyrocket and global stock markets to fall. The US has issued a "Depart Now" alert for its citizens in many Middle Eastern countries, highlighting the severe safety risks.

**Possible Long-Term Consequences:** Based on established trends, long-term consequences could include sustained volatility in global energy markets due to continued threats to shipping lanes and oil production. The conflict may further destabilize the Middle East, potentially leading to prolonged humanitarian crises and increased refugee flows. There is also a risk of increased cyber warfare and other forms of asymmetric conflict. Policy implications include intensified diplomatic efforts by international bodies to de-escalate, potential re-evaluation of defense postures by regional and international powers, and a shift in global alliances and trade routes if the Strait of Hormuz remains contested.

## SECTION 19 — This Day in History (Global)

- On March 3, 1845, Florida was admitted as the 27th U.S. state.
- On March 3, 1861, Alexander II of Russia signed the Emancipation Manifesto, freeing serfs.
- On March 3, 1956, Morocco gained its independence.
- On March 3, 1969, NASA launched Apollo 9 to test the lunar module.
- On March 3, 1974, Turkish Airlines Flight 981 crashed near Paris, France, killing all 346 aboard, making it the 4th highest death toll of any aviation accident at the time.

## SECTION 20 — This Day in History (India)

- On March 3, 1839, Jamsetji Nusserwanji Tata, the Indian pioneer industrialist and founder of Tata Group, was born.
- On March 3, 1939, Mohandas Gandhi began a fast in Bombay to protest autocratic rule in India.
- On March 3, 1947, India signed the United Nations Charter, signifying its commitment to global peace and cooperation.
- On March 3, 1949, Sarojini Naidu, the first female governor of India and a prominent freedom fighter, passed away.
- On March 3, 1707, Aurangzeb, the sixth Mughal emperor and last effective ruler of the Mughal Empire, died.

## SECTION 25 — Strategic Insight / Underreported Development

A significant but underreported trend is the dual nature of quantum computing advancements. While concerns exist about quantum computers potentially breaking current encryption standards, considerable research is simultaneously focusing on developing "quantum-safe" encryption systems and leveraging quantum mechanics for enhanced cybersecurity. This evolving race between quantum-enabled threats and quantum-resistant defenses highlights a critical, future-focused cybersecurity battleground that will profoundly impact national security, financial systems, and personal privacy.
